the_steeps.title: A soak at the Steeps
the_steeps.opening: You cover the door fee for two — that's the custom — and the attendant waves you both up the terraces. {name} tips {pos} head back to take in the tower: pools stacked into the dark, steam climbing like it has somewhere to be. “Okay,” {subj} says quietly. “Okay, this was a good call.”

beats.text: The first terrace is body-warm and green-lit. {name} sinks in to the collarbone and looks at you expectantly — your date, your move.

choices.text: Settle in beside them, shoulder to shoulder, and let the heat do the talking.
choices.reply: {Subj} exhales about a week's worth of tension. “You're quiet good company,” {subj} says, like {subj}'s deciding it right now.

choices.text: Cannonball. This is a bathhouse, not a church.
choices.reply: The wave hits {obj} square in the face. There's a silence with two possible endings — then {subj} laughs and starts planning revenge.

choices.text: Give them the tour first — you know which pools run hottest.
choices.reply: “You've done your homework,” {subj} says, trailing a hand in each pool you name, cataloguing the temperatures like promises.

beats.text: Three terraces up, the steam thickens and the city noise gives out entirely. The conversation drifts loose and unhurried, the way it only does when nobody can see anybody clearly.

choices.text: Talk training — bodies, limits, the good ache of getting stronger.
choices.reply: {Subj} stretches, considering. The topic either lands like a shared language or like homework — you can hear which in {pos} answer.

choices.text: Hum along with the pipes — the tower drones a low chord when the vents cycle.
choices.reply: {Subj} tilts {pos} head, catches the drone, and adds a fifth on top of your line — or doesn't. Either way {subj}'s watching you differently now.

choices.text: Say nothing. Listen to the water and let them fill the quiet if they want it.
choices.reply: For a while there's just water moving. Then {name} starts talking — really talking — the way people only do when nobody's pulling on the thread.

beats.text: The top terrace ends at the cold plunge: a black pool fed straight from the tower's shadow side. House rules say you don't leave without going in. {name} looks at it, then at you.

choices.text: Take their hand. Count of three. Go.
choices.reply: The cold hits like a verdict and you both come up yelling. {Subj} doesn't let go of your hand until the shivering stops, and maybe a little after.

choices.text: “You first. I'll judge your form.”
choices.reply: {Subj} narrows {pos} eyes at the challenge, and the plunge becomes a competition with scoring, appeals, and at least one disputed ruling.

choices.text: Pass. The warm pool has earned your loyalty.
choices.reply: {Subj} goes in alone, surfaces gasping, and paddles back to the warmth beside you. “Coward,” {subj} says, meaning something friendlier.

the_steeps.closing_good: You dry off slow, both of you pink and unhurried, hair curling in the heat. At the tower door {name} stops. “Next time,” {subj} says — not a question, an amendment to the schedule — and the walk back down through the Bloom feels shorter than the map says it is.
the_steeps.closing_flat: It was warm, anyway. {name} thanks you at the door, polite as a receipt, and the steam lets go of you both the moment you step outside.

night_market.title: Neon & broth at the Night Market
night_market.opening: The lot is in full bloom: biolights strung shoulder-high, six languages of haggling, steam ghosting off the stalls. {name} pockets {pos} hands and grins at the noise. “Feed me,” {subj} says, “and nobody gets hurt.”

beats.text: First stop is mandatory: food. The stalls offer skewered something, glow broth that lights from within, and the dare shelf — Vendor's Mercy, unlabeled, sold with a shrug.

choices.text: Glow broth for two — one bowl, two spoons, the vendor's knowing look for free.
choices.reply: The broth glows faint blue and so, briefly, do your teeth. {name} laughs at yours; you laugh at {pos}. The vendor has seen this exact scene a thousand times and still approves.

choices.text: Split a skewered something and agree never to ask which something.
choices.reply: It's delicious. It's absolutely delicious and you will both take the mystery of it to your graves, {subj} declares, licking a thumb.

choices.text: Vendor's Mercy. Two orders. Look them dead in the eye while you order.
choices.reply: It arrives smoking. Whether {name} lights up or blanches at the dare tells you exactly what kind of night {subj} was hoping for.

beats.text: You drift down the stall rows, shoulder to shoulder in the crowd-current. Tonight's rotating stalls flash their one-night-only wares under the biolights.

choices.text: Hunt the stalls for something small and exactly them — a gift, tonight only.
choices.reply: You find it three stalls in — small, cheap, and so precisely {obj} that {subj} goes quiet for a second before pocketing it like contraband.

choices.text: Invent lives for strangers in the crowd, trading chapters back and forth.
choices.reply: Your story about the ferry captain and the stolen orchid gets a sequel from {name} — or a bemused look. Either way the crowd becomes a library for a while.

choices.text: Follow the music to the far end, where somebody's rigged speakers to a fish cart.
choices.reply: The bass is terrible and the cart smells like the sea. {name} either starts moving to it anyway or grants it exactly one raised eyebrow — both are answers.

beats.text: At the broth stall on the way out, the vendor leans over the steam, conspiratorial: “You two, eh? Word travels.” The whole row is suddenly, theatrically busy not listening.

choices.text: Lean in and own it. “Word's slow. This is our second stall.”
choices.reply: The vendor howls. {name} colors to the ears and doesn't correct you — and {subj} walks a half-step closer the rest of the way out.

choices.text: Deflect it smoothly and steer the talk to the vendor's broth secrets.
choices.reply: The vendor, flattered, unveils exactly one secret (it's fish sauce). {name} watches you handle it and files something away, amused.

choices.text: Buy a static floss and stuff it in the vendor's mouth. Diplomatically.
choices.reply: The floss crackles. The vendor chews, electrified and delighted. {name} apologizes to the whole row on your behalf without meaning a word of it.

night_market.closing_good: The biolights thin out at the lot's edge and neither of you hurries. {name} is still glowing faintly from the broth, or possibly not from the broth. “Same time next rotation,” {subj} says, and takes the long way home.
night_market.closing_flat: The market swallows the evening whole. {name} thanks you at the lot's edge and heads off through the neon — fed, at least, if not exactly won.

gantry_9.title: Tea at the top of the city
gantry_9.opening: Nine floors of stairs and then the wind gives way to kettle-steam. {name} takes the rail-side table like it was reserved, the whole Grid laid out and blinking below. “So this is where you go,” {subj} says, deciding something about you.

beats.text: The chalkboard offers its usual conspiracies. The kettle-keeper waits, cloth over one shoulder, while {name} squints at tea names that explain nothing.

choices.text: Ask what they always order, then get it exactly right down to the honey.
choices.reply: {Subj} watches you relay the order — the little details {subj} didn't think anyone tracked — and forgets to look at the view for a while.

choices.text: Order for both of you, no hesitation. Confidence is a flavor.
choices.reply: The cups arrive. {name} sips, considers, and grants you a slow nod — the kind that costs something to earn.

choices.text: Two of whatever the chalkboard is calling 'hurry' tonight. Mystery builds character.
choices.reply: The tea tastes like a starter pistol. {name} blinks hard, laughs once, and grips the table like the gantry might take off.

beats.text: Steam off two cups, and below you the city runs its errands: Loop capsules threading the ring, cranes bowing over the docks, the Pit's light bleeding up through a grate in the Grid.

choices.text: Point out the exact spot where the two of you first met.
choices.reply: {Subj} leans over the rail to sight along your arm, and the look {subj} gives the spot — and then you — has a bookmark in it.

choices.text: Find their home from up here and learn what the neighborhood looks like small.
choices.reply: “That one,” {subj} says, tapping the air over a smudge of light, and tells you one thing about it {subj}'s never told anyone taller than this table.

choices.text: Trade guesses about which light below is having the worst night.
choices.reply: You build the poor devil a whole biography. {name} adds the tragic twist — or vetoes yours on procedural grounds. The city is briefly a novel.

beats.text: The wind picks up the way it does nine floors over everything, and the glass panes hum their one low note. {name} pulls {pos} sleeves down over {pos} hands.

choices.text: Move around the table to the wind side and give them your warm-side seat.
choices.reply: {Subj} objects for exactly one second, then takes the seat. The wind gets you instead, and it is, on balance, an excellent trade.

choices.text: Shoulder to shoulder, watch the city, and say absolutely nothing.
choices.reply: The kettles tick. The Grid blinks. {name} is warm along one whole side of you, and nobody ruins it by narrating.

choices.text: Keep talking so neither of you has to admit it's cold.
choices.reply: Your teeth chatter mid-sentence. {name} watches you power through it with the fascination of someone observing a stubborn machine.

gantry_9.closing_good: The last of the tea goes cold and honorably unfinished. On the stairs down, {name} stops at the fourth landing. “I'm keeping this,” {subj} says — the evening, {subj} means, or possibly the warm-side seat — and the city takes you both back a degree warmer than it let you go.
gantry_9.closing_flat: The tea was good; the wind was honest. {name} thanks you on the ground floor, already re-wrapped in the day {subj} stepped out of, and the gantry light clicks off its landing behind you.
